metadata.title: Supplemental Material for Tracking Emotions From Song Lyrics: Analyzing 30 Years of K-Pop Hits

abstract: Emotions that are shared by a large number of people could broadly impact affective experiences at the individual level. Here, we used text mining on popular song lyrics-a cultural product that has been suggested to mirror emotions that many members of a society value and prefer-to track the changes in emotions over time. Morpheme frequency analysis and structural topic modeling on 2,962 hit K-pop songs from 1990 to 2019 showed converging evidence for increased positive emotional content and decreased negative emotional content embedded within the lyrics. This pattern of temporal shift in emotions aligned with rapid changes in South Korea in the past 30 years, notably a rise in individualism and ego orientation in a traditionally collectivistic culture, as well as economic growth. More generally, this study illustrates a strategy for tracking emotions that people value and prefer from large natural language data, supplementing existing methods such as self-reported surveys and laboratory experiments.

sections.paper_id: Jo and Kim - 2023 - Tracking emotions from song lyrics Analyzing 30 years of K-pop hits.
paragraphs: Emotions are beyond personal, isolated experiences. An important aspect of emotion is its social and collective nature, corroborated by well-documented phenomena such as emotional contagion or group sentiment (Fowler & Christakis, 2008;Hatfield et al., 1993). Studies have found that emotions that people value and prefer at the sociocultural level are able to significantly impact emotional experiences and behavior at the individual level (Chim et al., 2018;Goldenberg et al., 2014;Moons et al., 2009;Sims et al., 2015;Tsai et al., 2006; see Tsai, 2017, for a review of cross-cultural comparisons of such effects). Therefore, measuring concurrent emotions that members of a society value and prefer, as well as tracking their changes over time, may provide a useful window into the affective states at an individual level. Music is a potent example that fits this description particularly well. Music elicits emotional response from the listeners following a common psychological principle found across cultures (Krumhansl, 2002). Moreover, music, especially hit songs that have garnered popularity in a society, can be usefully viewed as being reflective of the emotional preferences and experiences of its members (DeWall et al., 2011). As such, analyzing the musical features of popular songs that emerged as hits across time may offer a useful strategy in understanding the emotional characteristics at the sociocultural level. Traditionally, affective scientists have utilized methods such as self-report or experimental manipulations to measure and track changes in emotions (Tamir & Robinson, 2007;Yoon & Rottenberg, 2021). Complementing these classic sociological and psychological approaches, recent studies found success in extracting affective information by harnessing computational tools and large text-based databases-most notably, word analysis of Twitter data sets (Garcia & Rimé, 2019;Hsu et al., 2021;Smith et al., 2018).

Here, we attempted to analyze the affective information of song lyrics by adopting a similar computational approach to measure emotions that members of a society would likely value and prefer. The utility of song lyrics has been demonstrated in psychological research, where lyrics were used as a means to investigate withincultural differences (Snibbe & Markus, 2005). In particular, lyrics of a song that gained widespread popularity contain narratives and emotions that the public found to be attractive and acceptable. Indeed, prior research on popular U.S. songs from 1980 to 2007 analyzed the changes in the frequency of words appearing in lyrics and used it to gauge generational shifts in psychological traits and emotions (DeWall et al., 2011). Adopting a similar approach, we sought to extract affective information via modern computational text-mining tools that allow for advanced analyses of song lyrics beyond word frequency, such as topic modeling (Blei & Lafferty, 2009). Text mining refers to several techniques that utilize natural language processing (NLP) for extracting structured information from human language materials (Kwartler, 2017). Large amounts of natural language material are free form and unstructured, offering substantial challenges when attempting to extract the emotional contents within. Fortunately, with recent advances in text mining and NLP algorithms, measuring affective information from song lyrics has become an achievable feat.

In the present work, we focused on a specific genre of popular music referred to as Korean pop-now globally recognized as Kpop. Our rationale for choosing K-pop for the present report is based on the fact that K-pop has risen in popularity in the mainstream music scene, from the meteoric rise in popularity of Psy's "Gangnam Style" almost a decade ago to the #1 Billboard Hot 100 performance of BTS's "Butter" in 2021. K-pop is enjoying a more global reach than before, especially in Asian regions (Langit et al., 2019;Sutton, 2018), and thus the emotions expressed in K-pop have implications beyond South Korea. That is, we may be able to read a trail of emotional change at the cultural level, which may lay the groundwork for future research examining more international audiences.

Using text mining and NLP on 30 years of K-pop hit songs, we sought to answer the following research questions: What are the emotions observed in K-pop lyrics, and how do they change over time? Based on the idea that popular song lyrics may serve as a window into understanding cultural changes (DeWall et al., 2011), we focused on the rapid change in South Korea's cultural climate in the past 30 years-accentuated by a surge of individualism and ego orientation (Park et al., 2016), as well as economic growth. In this exploratory examination of K-pop hit songs and lyrics, we sought to investigate the changes in the appearance of words and topics conveying positive and negative emotions and how these temporal fluctuations mapped onto sociocultural and economic changes that emerged in the past 30 years.

sections.paper_id: Jo and Kim - 2023 - Tracking emotions from song lyrics Analyzing 30 years of K-pop hits.
paragraphs: We used lyrics from songs on the Melon's top 100 list for each year from 1990 to 2019 as our data. Melon is a music service provider comparable to Spotify or Apple Music that holds the dominant position in South Korea. According to Korea Creative Content Agency reports, Melon was the most popular music streaming service for Koreans from 2019 to 2021 and is used evenly by all ages (Kim, 2019(Kim, , 2020(Kim, , 2021). Melon's status in the Korean music market makes their top 100 lists good resources for K-pop's representative songs. We limited the scope of the data to the top 100 songs per year to use only the hit songs that permeated a given time period. By focusing on the top 100 chart, we sought to identify the dominant sentiment of the period through the songs that successfully attracted public attention. Melon also provides a lyrics database for the majority of the songs, which we utilized for further analyses. Of note, as Melon launched in 2004, the previous annual top 100 lists are Melon's estimates. Melon has been organizing annual charts prior to 2004 by analyzing the free-of-charge weekly music charts distributed to Korean record stores in the past, called the "Music Box Charts."

Of the 3,000 songs (100 songs per year for 30 years), we excluded 38 songs whose lyrics information were not available, yielding a grand total of 2,962 songs. See Table S1 in the online supplemental materials for a full list of songs included in the current analysis, sorted by year. The list is provided in Korean to preserve the original information for posterity. As our study aimed to look at changes in emotions on a year-by-year basis, a small number of missing songs were not expected to influence the results in a nontrivial manner. We note that if the same song has been listed on the top 100 list over multiple years, the lyrics of the same song may have been reflected in the analysis several times. This was a deliberate choice as a song being sufficiently popular for multiple years indicates that it aligned with the emotions that people valued and preferred for that time period. Out of 2,962 songs included in the analysis, a total of 215 songs have appeared in the annual top 100 lists more than once. Of these 215, 208 songs appeared twice, six songs appeared three times, and one song appeared five times. The song lyrics data are publicly available through the following link: https://www.melon.com/chart/age/index.htm?chartType=YE &chartGenre=KPOP&chartDate=2019.

sections.paper_id: Jo and Kim - 2023 - Tracking emotions from song lyrics Analyzing 30 years of K-pop hits.
paragraphs: We analyzed the characteristics and changes of emotional content present in song lyrics, first by tracking the frequency of the main adjectives and their fluctuations on a year-by-year basis. We chose to focus on adjectives as they are most likely to directly reveal emotions. If the frequency of words expressing negative emotions and words expressing positive sentiment vary over time, this would be a clue to tracking temporal fluctuations of emotions that people experience and/or prefer (Danner et al., 2001). We identified the most utilized adjectives in the entire song and highlighted key adjectives that are clearly associated with positive and negative sentiments (i.e., valence).

The procedure to identify the most utilized adjectives was as follows: We first calculated the frequency of adjectives by year. We identified the top appearing adjectives based on the frequency from each year's top 100 song lyrics and created a union set of these top 10 adjective lists over 30 years. We followed this procedure so as to not omit key adjectives that may have only appeared during a certain era, considering that the adjective usage may vary depending on the year.

We then categorized the sentiment of the adjectives by referencing a published Korean emotion vocabulary list (Sohn et al., 2012). Sohn et al. (2012) present a classification table of 504 emotion words created by 12 experts, which include Korean literature majors. If a word appears in Sohn et al. (2012) as positive sentiment (e.g., happiness, interest), we categorized it as positive. If a word appears in the list as negative sentiment (e.g., sadness, anger, fear, disgust, boredom, pain), we categorized it as negative. We note that our analysis also revealed adjectives that do not explicitly express or describe emotional states. As such words did not appear in the list, we classified them as neutral. Here, our strategy was to first identify adjectives that are widely used in song lyrics and then find words with clear emotional meaning. This was to ensure that This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

the adjectives included in the analysis (a) were clearly emotionally charged and (b) appeared frequently in song lyrics. Finally, we analyzed how the relative frequency of the key adjectives varied from year to year. We identified the proportion of each positive and negative adjective that frequently appeared in song lyrics each year in the entire adjectives and analyzed their changes across time.

sections.paper_id: Jo and Kim - 2023 - Tracking emotions from song lyrics Analyzing 30 years of K-pop hits.
paragraphs: Next, we used structural topic modeling (STM), a type of topic modeling that allowed us to infer core themes embedded within the lyrics. People express emotions not only in direct ways, such as using specific adjectives, but also indirectly through the use of specific subjects or materials. For example, a song about a breakup can be told to express negative emotions, even if its lyrics do not contain a single distinct negative adjective. To capture this indirect representation, we required techniques to capture topics present in human language materials. For this purpose, STM is a suitable method for estimating the topics from large language materials and estimating their temporal changes. STM estimates topics through statistical processes from large amounts of linguistic data, as do several topic-modeling techniques developed after latent Dirichlet allocation. The estimation is accomplished by defining a topic as a probability distribution over words and assuming that documents are generated from the topics and topic distributions per document, rather than human-written ones. STM estimates which topics and topic distributions are likely to generate the documents (Blei, 2012;Blei & Lafferty, 2009;Roberts et al., 2014). Therefore, the estimated topics and topic distributions per document provide crucial information.

As such, the main outputs from STM are topics, which are presented in the form of probability distributions over words that describe the data well. We note that the topics estimated by STM are not equal to the "topics" we usually think of. The latter is an intuitive subject; on the other hand, the former is a simple collection of numbers. However, the probability distributions over words (i.e., topics from STM) could provide essential information for inferring intuitive topics residing in the data (i.e., documents). When we read a document, we identify a topic by analyzing which words appear frequently and which appear together. For example, if words such as "ruling party," "opposition party," and "conflict" often appear together in an article, we infer that the topic of "political conflict" exists. By estimating the probability distribution over words with STM, we can efficiently identify topics even without reading the full extent of the documents. For example, a probability distribution over words could be estimated as follows: "breakup" À.01, "pain" À.01, "tears" À.009, "regret" À.008, etc. In this case, researchers may reason that breaking up a relationship and the resulting pain are important subjects and infer the topic of "the pain of breakup." Therefore, when multiple word probability distributions are estimated from a large volume of documents, we can estimate the topics residing within the text. Many studies have utilized topic-modeling methods, including STM, for identifying and analyzing topics in different types of documents, such as newspaper coverages and online forum posts (DiMaggio et al., 2013;Jo et al., 2022). In the present study, we also leveraged this approach and estimated 60 topics from popular song lyrics using STM.

This topic-modeling approach requires the researchers to manually interpret these topics. Since the topic that STM estimates is the probability distribution over words, it requires a humanunderstandable naming process. We interpreted individual topics with two types of information. The first was the main words for each topic. Words assigned with high probability are, of course, essential words in the topic. In addition, we used an indicator called FREX (frequency and exclusivity; Roberts et al., 2016). As an example, let us say there is a word assigned a high probability in every topic. Paradoxically, this word cannot provide information about a particular topic. Therefore, FREX identifies topic-specific keywords considering exclusivity with frequency. We identified the top 15 words on a probability basis and the top 15 words on a FREX basis by topic. The second type of information was the actual document (in this case, the lyrics of a song) with a high proportion of individual topics. As STM is able to also infer the topic distributions in each document, we could identify the actual documents where each topic accounts for the highest proportion. These documents show how each topic is realized as an actual language material. For example, if 90% of a document consists of Topic 2, and the rest of the topics have only a peripheral proportion, the document shows the realization of Topic 2 in language material. We identified five lyrics of each topic, in which each topic has the highest proportion. To summarize, using the aforementioned two types of information, both authors jointly interpreted and labeled each of the 60 topics.

Not only does STM infer topics, but it also infers changes in the proportion or content of topics based on the metadata of documents (Roberts et al., 2016(Roberts et al., , 2014)). In our case, we assumed that the proportion of topics in the lyrics would vary depending on which year the song lyrics were included in the Melon top 100 list. Information extracted from STM allowed us to explore the changes in expected topic proportions over time.

We then categorized the sentiments of each of the 60 topics identified from the previous step based on emotional valence. Again, these valenced topics may include those that do not directly describe specific emotional states. The classification work was discussed and carried out by both authors. We used three categories: (a) positive, (b) negative, and (c) neutral or otherwise unclassifiable. Decisions were made according to the authors' mutual agreement, going through the list on a topic-by-topic basis. We note that this procedure was done prior to any time-series analysis to minimize possible bias. To further rule out the possibility of potential researcher bias, we asked two colleagues who were blind to the hypotheses and conclusions of this study to code the valence of the topics. Along with the list of topics, we provided five actual song lyrics that contained the highest proportion of each topic. Valence coded by the authors was consistent with that by those who were blind to the study, with two exceptions; bodily sensation (breathing, heart, body) and body parts (lips, hair, eyes, etc.) were both coded as neutral rather than positive by those who were blind to the study. In light of this, we proceeded to remove these two topics from the list of positive topics. Pearson correlation was used to analyze the temporal dynamics of the proportions between the topics displaying positive and negative emotions.

We note that, of all word types in the song lyrics, only Korean nouns were submitted to STM. Our choice was motivated by two factors. First, recommendations from prior topic-modeling work show improved topic coherence by limiting the analysis to nouns This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly. (Martin & Johnson, 2015). Second, foreign words included in the lyrics usually do not convey significant meaning in Korean songs.

In addition, to ensure that nouns with little to no information did not influence STM performance, we only used nouns that appeared in at least two songs and lyrics containing at least two nouns. Following the preprocessing steps above, we ended up excluding six additional songs, yielding a total of 2,956 songs that were used in STM.

In short, we analyzed emotions embedded in the lyrics that were (a) expressed directly through frequency analysis of adjectives and (b) represented indirectly through estimating topics via STM. To this end, the Kakao Hangul Analyzer III, Khaiii, was used (https:// github.com/kakao/khaiii). Morphological analyzers are needed to accurately identify Korean words in song lyrics. Among several Korean morphological analyzers, we elected to use Khaiii as it is a recently developed, state-of-the-art deep-learning-based analyzer that utilizes convolutional neural network algorithms. Most of the remaining work used R (R Core Team, 2021) and its libraries. For aggregating song lyrics data from the Melon database, we used the following code: https://github.com/junhewk/melonchartlyrics. This study was not preregistered.

For example, a song about a breakup can be told to express negative emotions, even if its lyrics do not contain a single distinct negative adjective. sections.header_raw: Frequency and Valence of Main Adjectives
sections.header_norm: frequency and valence of main adjectives
paragraphs: We identified the 27 most commonly used adjectives throughout the period (M = 883; minimum-maximum = 38-6,704 appearances), following mentioned procedure. Initially, we identified 29 words. However, we detected incorrectly divided words due to the limitation of the tokenizing performance of the Khaiii morpheme analyzer. We excluded incorrectly tokenized words. The full list of adjectives and their frequencies are shown in Table 1. These adjectives were categorized as positive (n = 2), negative (n = 6), or neutral (n = 19), based on the Korean emotion vocabulary list (Sohn et al., 2012). As we were interested in emotionally charged words, we proceeded to analyze only the valenced (i.e., positive or negative) adjectives. Changes in the relative frequency of adjectives by year are shown in Figure 1.

To further quantify and confirm the temporal shift in the proportion of adjectives, we computed Pearson correlation coefficients between the relative frequency of adjectives and time (see Table 2). Since a total of eight adjectives (two positive and six negative) were tested separately, a false discovery rate (FDR) correction was imposed on the significance threshold (q , .05) to correct for multiple statistical tests (Benjamini & Hochberg, 1995). Both words expressing positive emotions, "good": r(28) = .8, p , .001; "pretty": r(28) = .57, p = .001, showed a significant positive correlation with time. Two out of six words conveying negative emotions, "sad": r(28) = À.74, p , .001; "lonely": r(28) = À.49, p = .007, demonstrated a significant negative correlation with time, while two words, "hurtful": r(28) = .42, p = .021; "bad": r(28) = .45, p = .012, showed the opposite pattern.

paragraphs: Using STM, we extracted 60 topics from 2,956 song lyrics. Both authors interpreted and named each topic. We initially excluded 13 topics that were not interpretable. Then, each topic was categorized as positive (n = 16), negative (n = 9), or neutral or otherwise unclassifiable (n = 22) in terms of their association with valence (see Table S2 in online supplemental materials for a full list). Again, we focused our analysis on the 25 topics with identifiable positive or negative valence. Average proportion of the topics (see Figure 2) as well as their temporal changes (see Figure 3) are summarized.

To further quantify and confirm the trends of these topics over time, we computed the Pearson correlation coefficients between the expected proportion of topics and time (see Figure 4). We a The term "beautiful" in Korean is often used to describe inner beauty, dealing with immaterial and metaphysical aspects of an object. The Korean term for "pretty," in contrast, is almost exclusively used to describe the physical appearance of an object. Although the apparent meaning of these two words is similar, the gap in the nuance is quite substantial. "Pretty" is usually reserved to describe an object that is pleasing-positive valence-to the senses (e.g., eyes). "Beautiful," on the other hand, can be used to describe something that is not typically considered to be of positive valence. For example, in Korean language, it is not uncommon to describe a mutual breakup between lovers to be "beautiful." A beautiful heartbreak is a term often used and understood in Korean-but neither breakups nor heartbreaks are ever described as "pretty." The adjective "beautiful" in Korean is more difficult to simply categorize in terms of valence, due to its diverse contextdependent usage, compared to "pretty." We speculate such contrast between "beautiful" versus "pretty" in Korean is what contributed to the former not being coded as a positive word in the Korean emotion vocabulary (Sohn et al., 2012), which we used to classify the adjectives in our study.

found that four negative topics showed significant negative correlations with time, whereas one negative topic exhibited an opposite pattern. On the other hand, eight positive topics showed significant positive correlations with time, while five positive topics displayed an opposite pattern (all FDR-corrected qs , .05). Finally, to summarize the associations among valenced adjectives, topics, and time, correlations between the average proportion of positive/negative topics and the average proportion of positive/negative adjectives were computed (see Table 3). Overall, the proportion of positive adjectives and topics increased with time, whereas negative topics decreased with time. Negative adjectives were not correlated with time. In addition, positive adjectives were strongly correlated with positive/negative topics. Positive and negative topics were inversely correlated (all FDR-corrected qs , .05). In contrast, no significant correlations were observed between negative adjectives and negative topics.

sections.paper_id: Jo and Kim - 2023 - Tracking emotions from song lyrics Analyzing 30 years of K-pop hits.
paragraphs: A comprehensive analysis of K-pop hit songs from 1990 to 2019 showed a general pattern of temporal shift in the emotional content embedded within the lyrics. Conversely, the proportion of topics that convey negative emotions showed a decreasing trend during the same time period.

Text mining of song lyrics revealed a general pattern of temporal change of the emotions that people are collectively exposed to. It could be understood that a hit song permeates the society and exerts substantial influence over its members during the time when it has gained sufficient popularity (Kresovich, 2022). Of course, the relationship between popular music and society is likely to be bidirectional and reciprocal as it may be just as likely that the overall mood of the society influences songs conveying certain Note. types of emotions to gain greater popularity. Nonetheless, our findings imply that the emotional valence that people are exposed to and/or prefer has changed over time. The formation of a hit song is not independent of people's reactions or responses. Rather, by definition, a song becomes a hit only when a large number of people sympathize with the emotion that it conveys or show synchronized affective responses to it-consistent with a general definition of collective emotions (Niedenthal & Brauer, 2012;Von Scheve & Ismer, 2013). The present findings show a general pattern of temporal change in the emotional contents of K-pop song lyrics such that the use of positive adjectives and topics increased, whereas negative topics decreased in the past 30 years. Typically, negative emotions (e.g., fear, anxiety) are associated with avoidance motivation (Carver, 2005), whereas positive emotions (e.g., happiness, joy) invoke approach motivation (Cacioppo et al., 1999). Anger is an exception to this distinction as this negative emotion is often associated with approach motivation, a characteristic shared with many positive emotions (Harmon-Jones et al., 2014). Perhaps, then, it may be the approach-motivation-related aspect of anger that is being reflected in our data such that an increasing, rather than decreasing, trend is observed.

It is worth noting that prior research on popular songs from the United States and the United Kingdom has revealed a general pattern that is the opposite from South Korea: a decrease in positive emotions and an increase in negative emotions over time (DeWall et al., 2011;Interiano et al., 2018). In the United States, for example, a quantitative analysis of hit pop song lyrics (defined as the top 10 songs according to the Billboard Hot 100 chart) from 1980 to 2007 showed a monotonic decrease in positive emotion words, paired with a monotonic increase in angry or antisocial words (DeWall et al., 2011). Interestingly, this was interpreted as being reflective of escalating individualistic traits (e.g., narcissistic traits) during the same time period in the United States (Twenge & Foster, 2010). This is of particular relevance as we also believe the rise of individualism and ego orientation in South Korea (Park et al., 2016) places our findings from K-pop in the proper context, Note. mirroring the temporal shift in the emotional content found within the lyrics. In this vein, the general increase in positive emotions and decrease in negative emotions may also reflect a tendency to prefer clearly positive versus mixed affective states, which is represented as the desire to maximize the positive and minimize the negative often found in individualistic cultures like the United States (Heine et al., 1999;Sims et al., 2015). Discrepancies in the time periods notwithstanding, these seemingly conflicting observations may be reconciled by considering cultural differences: that the United States boasts a highly individualistic culture, whereas South Korea is a traditionally collectivistic culture (Hofstede, 1984). The surge of individualistic traits, then, should be viewed differently across cultures as it may lead to divergent outcomes. For instance, in an already individualistic culture like the United States, we can speculate that further increase in individualistic traits may exacerbate the adverse aspects of individualism (e.g., rise in narcissistic traits). By the same token, it is not implausible to think that beneficial features of individualism emerge as individualistic traits increase, at least initially, in a collectivistic culture such as South Korea. Individualistic values being introduced to a collectivistic society could encourage people to pursue their individual goals and life course, while distancing themselves against potentially excessive obligation to the community. This could enhance happiness and subjective well-being at the individual level (Diener et al., 1995). We speculate that this effect is less likely to be produced in an already individualistic society because its people are expected to already possess more freedom to pursue their individual goals. On the other hand, unlike the United States, the coexistence of rising individualism and traditional collectivism found in contemporary East Asian cultures such as Japan has been suggested to exert negative impact on interpersonal relationships (Ogihara, 2017). This demonstrates that increases in individualism may have different psychosocial ramifications depending on whether the culture was individualistic to begin with (e.g., United States, United Kingdom) or traditionally collectivistic (e.g., South Korea, Japan). Future studies could expand on this idea and systematically explore the differential effects of rising individualism by culture.

Given that music and song lyrics are considered as cultural products that record changes in culture across time (Morling & Lamoreaux, 2008), differential effects of increased individualistic traits may be reflected in a 30-year trajectory of popular U.S. songs versus K-pop. This possibility is further corroborated by a recent analysis of popular songs released in the United Kingdom-another highly individualistic culture-from 1985 to 2015, in which highlevel musical features such as happiness and brightness showed a downward trend in the span of 30 years (Interiano et al., 2018). This evidence is particularly potent as the musical features were extracted using machine-learning algorithms, completely independent of lyrical information.

As pervasive as the individualism-collectivism dimension is in characterizing cultural differences (Markus & Kitayama, 1991), it likely would not be the sole factor that accounts for the changes in the sentiment embedded within popular songs. As such, we believe considering other potential factors that might provide alternative explanation of the present findings would be helpful. Here, we consider the impact of economic growth. During the 30-year period we examined, South Korea experienced continuous economic growth. Of note, individual purchasing power and gross domestic product have increased within that time window (Organization for Economic Cooperation & Development, 2022). We speculate that this economic growth can also serve as the background for increasing feelings of and/or preference for positive emotions, which is in line with the predictions from the reserve capacity model (Gallo & Matthews, 2003) based on observations in the United States (Diener et al., 2013;Gallo et al., 2005). Greater purchasing power could be linked to more positive emotions experienced, through the realization of individual desires. Of course, it is also possible that this increase in purchasing power is a third variable that acts to promote both individualism and positive emotions. Based on the exploratory nature of the present study, future research may be able to delineate the association between economic growth, individualism, and positive emotions.

Recent developments in text mining and NLP have presented new possibilities for affective science. In this study, we provide a means to perform a long-term analysis of emotional content from song lyrics, which maintain a stable format for decades. The broader implications from the present findings are that affective information can be obtained from any text-based cultural product with large, archived natural language data (e.g., tweets, online message boards posts, podcast transcripts). Above all, text mining can serve as a useful research tool to supplement existing methods for measuring emotion and affect at a societal level.

The broader implications from the present findings are that affective information can be obtained from any text-based cultural product with large, archived natural language data (e.g., tweets, online message boards posts, podcast transcripts). Above all, text mining can serve as a useful research tool to supplement existing methods for measuring emotion and affect at a societal level.
sections.preview_head: A comprehensive analysis of K-pop hit songs from 1990 to 2019 showed a general pattern of temporal shift in the emotional content embedded within the lyrics. Both morpheme frequency analysis and STM yielded converging results: The proportion of adjectives and topics that express positive emotions showed an increasing trend in the past 30 years. Conversely, the proportion of topics that convey negative emotions showed a decreasing trend during the same time period.

Future studies would benefit from considering both text and nontext features of music. Second, while K-pop has indeed grown fast in recent years, it is worth noting that the primary audience of the many songs included in the current analysis has been Koreans. Thus, the generalizability of the findings we report here could be addressed in future research. Third, K-pop popularity charts and rankings are not impervious to the external influences such as larger labels, production companies, and fan bases (Im et al., 2018;Shin & Park, 2018). sections.paper_id: Jo and Kim - 2023 - Tracking emotions from song lyrics Analyzing 30 years of K-pop hits.
paragraphs: In summary, our results shed light on the changes in the overall pattern of emotions that people are collectively exposed to, as well as the changes in the emotions that people value, prefer, and share with others. An in-depth analysis of 30 years of K-pop songs suggests an increase in positive emotions and a decrease in negative emotions embedded within the lyrics. This temporal shift in emotions coincided with the rise of individualistic traits and ego orientation and economic growth in South Korea. As a cultural product that mirrors the psyche (Morling & Lamoreaux, 2008), song lyrics have the potential to be a useful resource for tracking emotions. Tracking the temporal changes in emotions is of particular importance as emotions at a societal level (e.g., fear and anxiety) can be invoked by historical events (e.g., COVID-19 pandemic) and heavily influence individual behavior (e.g., hoarding daily necessities) that may have sociocultural ramifications (e.g., national economy). As such, accurately measuring and analyzing emotions is essential to explaining and predicting population behavior and changes in public opinion. Given that natural language data can only accumulate as a function of time and various databases are constantly growing in size, this study offers a strategy that can assist existing methods for tracking emotions by utilizing morpheme frequency analysis and STM.
